behaviors.description: Plugin with missing required manifest field is rejected before any plugin code is executed
behaviors.justification: Manifest validation is pure JSON schema validation logic. The failure mode—rejecting invalid manifests before module load—can be verified by mocking the filesystem to return a manifest missing a required field, then asserting the loader throws an error and never calls require(). The core concern (validate-then-load ordering) doesn't require real I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the filesystem to return a plugin manifest missing a required field (e.g., 'name' or 'version'). Invoke the loader and assert it throws a validation error before ever calling require() for that plugin's module.

behaviors.description: When plugin A depends on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's
behaviors.justification: Dependency resolution and hook invocation order are pure deterministic algorithms (topological sort). Unit tests with mocked onLoad hooks can verify the loader correctly computes and respects dependency order. The algorithm doesn't require real I/O, filesystem, or external systems.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugins: B with no dependencies, and A that explicitly depends on B. Mock their onLoad hooks to track invocation order. Invoke the loader and assert B.onLoad() is called before A.onLoad().

behaviors.description: Circular dependencies between plugins are detected and neither plugin loads
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection in a dependency graph is pure deterministic graph algorithm, testable without real I/O. A unit test can supply manifests with a circular dependency (A → B → A) and verify the loader detects the cycle and rejects both plugins without executing any onLoad hooks.
behaviors.test_description: Create manifests where plugin A depends on B and plugin B depends on A. Invoke the loader and assert it throws a cycle-detection error. Verify neither plugin's onLoad hook is ever called.

behaviors.description: Plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the entry module has been imported and executed
behaviors.justification: The execution order (require() before onLoad()) is a control-flow concern. A unit test can mock require() and onLoad() to track call sequence and verify require() is invoked first. However, to fully confirm the module code actually executes (not just that require() is called), Integration testing with real plugin modules and observable side effects is more robust. Unit-level mocking catches ordering bugs but may miss subtle execution failures.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the module loader and onLoad callback. Call loader.load(plugin) and assert require() is called before onLoad() is invoked. For stronger verification, use a real test plugin module that sets a observable side effect (e.g., a module-level variable), and assert that side effect is present before onLoad() runs.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error in its onLoad hook, remaining plugins that do not depend on it continue to load
behaviors.justification: Error isolation and continuation logic is pure control-flow. Unit tests with mocked onLoad hooks can verify the loader catches an exception from one plugin and continues loading others that have no dependency on it. No real I/O, external systems, or unpredictable behavior required.
behaviors.test_description: Create three mock plugins A, B, C where B does not depend on A. Configure A's onLoad hook to throw an error. Invoke the loader and assert B and C load successfully, while an error is captured/logged for A.
